GPU-Collateralized Debt Facilities: The Rise of Chip Loans
Bryan Gibson  ·  Brandon Hall  ·  Peter Demerjian   |   Interim Research Presentation · April 2026
Research Question
Do existing accounting standards adequately measure the depreciation, revenue recognition, and leverage risk embedded in GPU-collateralized debt?
Problem Setting
  • $35B in GPU-backed debt originated 2023–2025 with no accounting precedent and no secondary market for liquidation
  • Vendor-financing collapses (Lucent, GE Capital) show collateral value risk is systematic, not idiosyncratic
  • Lenders price covenant compliance on EBITDA metrics management can shift via useful-life elections
Literature Gap
  • GPU useful life is currently empirically unverified — ASC 360-10 was written for assets with secondary markets
  • No prior study examines ASC 360 / 606 / 842 interaction in technology-collateralized debt
  • BIS (2026) flags AI-financing as an emerging macro-financial risk with no measurement framework
$35B
GPU-backed facilities committed
Zero in Aug 2023 · 3 years
$2.7B
Annual depreciation gap · 2 vs 6 yr
Exceeds EBITDA on same asset base
–83%
H100 rental decline · 18 mo
No secondary GPU market exists
CoreWeave · The Case Study (FY2025 10-K)
$21.6B
Total debt · 12 instruments
–$7.2B
Free cash flow FY2025
$38.5B
Uncommenced leases (ASC 842)
–0.04×
EBIT / interest coverage
Conceptual Framing
  • Watts & Zimmerman (1986) Positive Accounting Theory: managers select useful-life estimates to maximize EBITDA and satisfy DSCR covenant thresholds
  • Shleifer & Vishny (1992) asset specificity: GPU collateral has no deep secondary market · industry-wide distress produces near-zero liquidation recovery
  • Jensen & Meckling (1976) agency costs of debt: information asymmetry between lenders and borrowers on collateral value enables systematic understatement of leverage
Research Method
  • Single case study: CoreWeave FY2025 10-K, S-1, 8-K covenant amendments
  • Scenario analysis across 2yr / 4yr / 6yr useful-life elections · ROIC vs. 11.13% WACC
  • Lease capitalization: implied borrowing rate applied to $38.5B uncommenced leases
Selected References  ·  BIS Bulletin 120: Private credit and AI financing (2026)  ·  CoreWeave S-1; 10-Q Filings; 8-K Covenant Amendments (Dec 2025)  ·  Lazonick & March (2011). GPU-Collateralized Debt Facilities: Capital Flow & Scenario Analysis
Bryan Gibson  ·  Brandon Hall  ·  Peter Demerjian   |   Supplemental Analysis · April 2026
Useful-Life Scenario Analysis · CoreWeave FY2025
ElectionAnnual D&AAdj. EBITDAROICvs WACC
2-Year$10.8B–$3.2B–14.2%–25.4pp
4-Year$5.4B$2.2B–2.1%–13.2pp
6-Year FILED$3.6B$4.0B+1.8%–9.3pp
WACC: 11.13% · All scenarios destroy value vs. capital cost
Lease Capitalization (ASC 842)
  • $38.5B uncommenced leases excluded from balance sheet under ASC 842 non-commenced exception
  • Implied borrowing rate 8.2% applied → $31.6B present value liability understated
  • Adjusted net-debt-to-EBITDA: 4.8× reported → 12.3× restated
  • DSCR covenants breach under all capitalization scenarios
Methodology · Case Study Design
  • Yin (2009) single-case embedded design: CoreWeave as critical case — largest pure-play GPU lessee
  • Primary: 10-K, S-1, 8-K filings; secondary: Jon Peddie GPU market data, BIS macro data
  • Triangulation: accounting scenario model × market pricing × covenant compliance simulation
Theoretical Contributions
  • Extends PAT (Watts & Zimmerman 1986) to technology-collateralized debt: useful-life accrual becomes the primary covenant management lever
  • ASC 606 × 718 interaction: AMD warrant grants to Meta and OpenAI constitute consideration payable to a customer — upper-bound revenue reduction $10.3B (32% of recognized revenue); FASB has not acted
  • Operationalizes Shleifer-Vishny (1992) fire-sale discount for GPU secondary markets — no secondary market means recovery approaches zero
  • First application of Jensen-Meckling agency cost framework to AI infrastructure financing
Implications for Standard-Setters
  • FASB ASC 360-10 useful-life guidance written for plant & equipment — no provision for assets with empirically unverified depreciation cycles
  • ASC 842 non-commenced exception creates a disclosure gap material to covenant compliance assessment
  • ASC 606 × 718 interaction on vendor warrant grants remains unaddressed — billions in recognized revenue at risk across the GPU ecosystem
